schools.summary: The binding-angel: 'and I saw an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand' — an angel descending with the key of the abyss and a great chain, to bind and imprison the dragon (vv.2-3, the instruments marking his commission to restrain Satan); 'the key of the bottomless pit' authority to open and shut the abyss (the same abyss from which the locusts and beast arose, 9:1; 11:7, here the prison into which Satan is cast); 'a great chain in his hand' the binding-chain (the figure of the restraining, binding of Satan, the great chain signifying the mighty restraint laid on him) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Victorinus and Bede read the angel's binding of Satan with the great chain as the restraint of the devil's power within the framework of the thousand years (vv.2-7); the great chain signifies the mighty restraint laid on Satan, the binding by which his deceiving of the nations is curbed during the appointed period — the patristic reading that frames the millennium as the time of Satan's binding.

themes: an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand
the angel with the key of the abyss and a great chain
the angel commissioned to bind and imprison the dragon
the instruments of restraining Satan

schools.summary: The binding of Satan: 'and he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years' — the angel seizing the dragon, identified (as 12:9) as the old serpent, the Devil, and Satan (the great adversary laid hold of and bound); Barnes and the spiritual/amillennial reading (Bede, after Augustine) take the binding as the restraint of Satan's power during a long period — the gospel age — in which his deceiving of the nations is curbed (v.3), the 'thousand years' taken symbolically of a long, complete period; the premillennial/chiliast reading (Gill, Victorinus, the futurist) takes the binding as a future imprisonment of Satan for a literal thousand years, during which Christ and the risen saints reign on earth (vv.4-6, the literal millennium) — the nature of the binding part of the great millennial crux (v.4).

Bede (amillennial, after Augustine) reads the 'thousand years' as a symbol of the gospel age — the present reign of Christ and his church, during which Satan is bound/restrained from wholly deceiving the nations; Victorinus (chiliast) takes the thousand years and the binding as relating to a coming era. The binding of Satan curbs his power for the appointed time, whether the present church age (Bede) or a future millennium (Victorinus).

themes: he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years
the binding of Satan for a thousand years
the restraint of the adversary for the appointed period
part of the great millennial crux

schools.summary: Satan sealed in the abyss: 'and cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him, that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season' — Satan cast into the abyss, shut up, and sealed (the threefold action — cast, shut, sealed — marking his secure, complete imprisonment, wholly restrained); the purpose 'that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled' (to stop Satan's deception of the nations for the period, in the amillennial reading the restraint of his deceiving power during the gospel age, in the premillennial his removal during the literal millennium, the nations freed from his deception for the thousand years); 'after that he must be loosed a little season' (when the thousand years end, Satan released for a short time, vv.7-9, to make a final assault before his final doom, the binding not yet his final destruction) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him
that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season
Satan sealed in the abyss, to deceive no more for the thousand years
the brief final loosing before the end

schools.summary: The premillennial/chiliast reading: 'and I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus... and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years' — Gill (with Victorinus and the futurist) takes it literally: the martyrs and saints bodily raised (the first resurrection, v.5) and reigning with Christ on earth for a literal thousand years; the dead literally raised from their graves to reign on earth with Christ during the millennium, the saints sharing in his rule, the reign the reward of the faithful witnesses who refused the beast and his mark (ch. 13).

Barnes takes the 'living and reigning' figuratively — not a bodily resurrection but the revival of the spirit and cause of the martyrs (the principles for which they died becoming again the reigning, pervading spirit on earth, true religion becoming the dominant power among men, a revival/postmillennial reading of the first resurrection). The amillennial reading (Bede, after Augustine; Ellicott notes it as 'one of the keys') takes the souls of the martyrs as living and reigning with Christ in heaven during the present gospel age, the 'first resurrection' being spiritual (the new birth, or the souls of the dead reigning with Christ in the intermediate state), the thousand years symbolic. Robertson and Ellicott note the literal-vs-spiritual divide as the crux of the chapter.

Victorinus (chiliast) reads the thousand-year reign of the risen saints with Christ as a coming era; Bede (amillennial, after Augustine) reads it as the present reign of Christ with his church and the souls of the saints during the gospel age. The martyrs who refused the beast, his image, and his mark reign with Christ, the reign the reward of the faithful — whether on earth in a future millennium (Victorinus) or in heaven during the church age (Bede).

debates.question: What is the thousand-year reign of the saints with Christ (and the 'first resurrection')?

sides.position: Premillennial: a literal bodily resurrection of the martyrs/saints, who reign with Christ on earth for a literal thousand years before the general resurrection

Amillennial: the souls of the martyrs reigning with Christ in heaven during the present gospel age; the 'first resurrection' spiritual (regeneration or the souls in the intermediate state); the thousand years symbolic
Figurative/revival (postmillennial-tending): the revival of the martyrs' cause and spirit — true religion becoming the reigning power on earth

themes: I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them
the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus... lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years
the martyrs reigning with Christ a thousand years
the central crux of the millennium (premillennial vs amillennial vs revival)

schools.summary: The first resurrection: 'but the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection' — over against those who reigned (v.4), the rest of the dead not living again until the thousand years ended (a distinction drawn between the reigning saints and the rest, whose living again is delayed); the premillennial reading (Gill, Victorinus) takes it of a literal distinction between two bodily resurrections — the saints raised first (the first resurrection), the rest/wicked raised at the end (the general resurrection, vv.12-13); the amillennial reading (Bede; Ellicott) takes the 'living again' of the rest as their bodily resurrection at the last day, while the first resurrection of the reigning saints is spiritual (regeneration, or the souls reigning with Christ); 'This is the first resurrection' — the reigning of the saints called the first resurrection in distinction from the second (the general resurrection, v.12), whether literal/bodily (premillennial) or spiritual/figurative (amillennial, or Barnes's revival) the heart of the millennial debate (v.4) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished
This is the first resurrection
the first resurrection and the rest of the dead
the nature of the first resurrection the heart of the millennial debate

schools.summary: The blessing on the partakers: 'blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years' — those who share in the first resurrection (v.5) pronounced blessed and holy (the fifth of the seven beatitudes of Revelation, to have part in the first resurrection being blessedness and holiness); 'on such the second death hath no power' (the second death, the lake of fire, v.14; 21:8, having no power over those in the first resurrection, they secure from eternal death, their salvation sure); 'they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years' (the partakers priests of God and Christ, 1:6; 5:10, and reigning with him, whether in the literal millennial reign — premillennial, Gill — or the present/heavenly reign — amillennial, Bede — or the reign of revived religion — Barnes, their blessed, priestly, royal estate with Christ) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power
they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years
the blessing on those in the first resurrection
the fifth beatitude: blessed, secure, priestly, and reigning

schools.summary: Satan loosed: 'and when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison' — at the end of the thousand years, Satan released from his imprisonment (v.3, the binding for the period only, his loosing preceding the final assault and his final doom, vv.8-10); 'the thousand years are expired' (the millennial period, whether the gospel age or a literal millennium, coming to its end, the time of Satan's restraint over); 'Satan shall be loosed out of his prison' (Satan freed for 'a little season,' v.3, to make a last deception and assault, the loosing testing and revealing, and leading to his final destruction, the brief, final outbreak of evil before the end) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison
Satan loosed at the end of the thousand years
the adversary freed for the final assault
the brief, final outbreak of evil before the end

schools.summary: Satan gathering Gog and Magog: 'and shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea' — released, Satan resuming his deceiving of the nations (which the binding had stopped, v.3), gathering them from the four corners of the earth (the worldwide, final deception); 'Gog and Magog' (drawn from Ezekiel 38-39, the great enemy from the north) the symbolic name for the final, vast host of the enemies of God (whether literal future nations or a symbol of the gathered powers of evil in the last assault, the figure of the comprehensive, final hostility against God's people); 'to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea' (an innumerable host mustered for the final battle against the saints, the vast multitude marking the magnitude of the last assault, the final, futile war against God's people) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog
to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea
Satan gathering Gog and Magog to the final battle
the final, innumerable muster against God's people

schools.summary: The assault crushed by fire: 'and they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city: and fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them' — the host (Gog and Magog) spreading over the earth and surrounding the camp of the saints and the beloved city (the church, or Jerusalem as the figure of God's people, the final siege of God's people by the gathered enemies); 'the camp of the saints... and the beloved city' the church (God's beloved people, gathered as in a camp or holy city, the object of the final assault); 'fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them' (before any battle, fire from heaven devouring the besieging host, as 2 Kings 1:10; Ezekiel 38:22; 39:6, the final assault crushed not by the saints' arms but by God's direct judgment, the sudden, total destruction of the last enemies) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city
fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them
the assault on the saints destroyed by fire from heaven
the final enemies crushed by God's direct judgment

schools.summary: The devil's final doom: 'and the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are, and shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever' — Satan, the deceiver, at last cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and false prophet were already cast (19:20, the unholy trinity meeting its final, common doom, the devil's everlasting destruction); 'where the beast and the false prophet are' (Satan joining the beast and false prophet, 19:20, in the lake of fire, the three united in their final punishment, the complete overthrow of the antichristian powers and their author); 'shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever' (the punishment unceasing and everlasting, the devil, beast, and false prophet suffering eternal torment, the final, irreversible doom of the powers of evil, the certainty and perpetuity of their judgment) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are
shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever
the devil cast into the lake of fire forever
the everlasting destruction of the powers of evil

schools.summary: The great white throne: 'and I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them' — a great white throne appearing, with one seated on it (God, or Christ as judge, the white marking the purity and justice of the judgment, the throne of the final, general judgment of all the dead, vv.12-15); 'a great white throne' the throne of judgment (great in majesty, white in spotless purity and justice, the seat of the final, righteous judgment of the world); 'from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them' (before the face of the Judge, the present earth and heaven fleeing away and vanishing, cf. 21:1; 2 Peter 3:10, the old creation dissolving or removed at the final judgment, the awful majesty of the Judge before whom the cosmos cannot stand) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Ellicott.

themes: I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it
from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them
the great white throne of final judgment
the awful throne before which the cosmos flees

schools.summary: The dead judged by their works: 'and I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works' — all the dead, of every rank (small and great), standing before God for judgment (the general, universal judgment of all mankind, none exempt); 'the books were opened' (the record of men's deeds) 'and another book... which is the book of life' (the register of the saved) — two kinds of books: the books recording works, and the book of life recording the redeemed, the judgment proceeding from both; 'the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works' (the dead judged according to their works as recorded in the books, the principle of judgment by works, Romans 2:6; Matthew 16:27, each judged by what he has done, the books bearing witness) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life
the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works
the dead judged out of the books according to their works
the universal judgment by the record of deeds

schools.summary: The dead given up for judgment: 'and the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them: and they were judged every man according to their works' — the sea, death, and hades surrendering all their dead (all the dead, wherever they died or were held, brought forth for judgment, the comprehensive, universal resurrection of all the dead to judgment); 'the sea... and death and hell (hades)' (every realm that held the dead — the sea, the grave, the abode of departed spirits — yielding them up, none missing, all summoned to the judgment); 'they were judged every man according to their works' (each judged individually by his works, v.12; Romans 2:6, the repetition stressing the principle of judgment by deeds, every man receiving according to what he has done) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them
they were judged every man according to their works
the sea, death, and hell giving up their dead for judgment
the universal resurrection to judgment by works

schools.summary: Death and hell cast into the lake of fire: 'and death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death' — death and hades themselves cast into the lake of fire (death, the last enemy, abolished, 1 Corinthians 15:26, the very powers of death and the grave destroyed, the lake of fire 'the second death'); 'death and hell (hades) were cast into the lake of fire' (death and the grave, having given up their dead, themselves destroyed, the abolition of death and hades marking the end of their reign, no more death for the redeemed, 21:4); 'This is the second death' (the lake of fire defined as the second death, cf. 21:8; 2:11; 20:6, the final, eternal death as distinct from the first/bodily death, the second death the eternal perdition of the lost in the lake of fire) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death
death and hell cast into the lake of fire, the second death
the last enemy destroyed, death itself abolished
the lake of fire the final, eternal death

schools.summary: The doom of the lost: 'and whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire' — all whose names are not written in the book of life (v.12; 13:8) cast into the lake of fire (the final sentence on the lost, the book of life the decisive register, those not found in it sharing the second death); 'not found written in the book of life' (the book of life, the register of the redeemed, the test, whoever is not recorded in it condemned, the absence of one's name in the book of life sealing the doom); 'was cast into the lake of fire' (the lost cast into the lake of fire, the second death, v.14, the final, eternal perdition of all who are not Christ's, the solemn close of the judgment scene, before the new heaven and new earth) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire
the doom of those not in the book of life
the book of life the decisive register
the final, eternal perdition of all not Christ's
